passages.explanation: Caesar Augustus issues a decree that the entire Roman world should be registered — a census. Everyone goes to their own town to register. Joseph goes from Nazareth in Galilee to Bethlehem in Judea, the town of David, because he belongs to the house and line of David. He goes with Mary, who is pledged to him and expecting a child. While they're there, the time comes for the baby to be born. She gives birth to her firstborn son, wraps him in cloths, and places him in a manger, because there's no guest room available for them. Luke places Jesus' birth within world history — the most powerful man on earth issues a decree, and it unknowingly fulfils a prophecy about Bethlehem (Micah 5:2). The King of the universe arrives in an animal's feeding trough.

Shepherds are living out in the fields nearby, keeping watch over their flocks at night. An angel of the Lord appears, the glory of the Lord shines around them, and they're terrified. The angel says: don't be afraid. I bring you good news that will cause great joy for all the people. Today in the town of David a Saviour has been born to you — he is the Messiah, the Lord. This will be a sign: you'll find a baby wrapped in cloths and lying in a manger. Suddenly a great company of the heavenly host appears, praising God: glory to God in the highest heaven, and on earth peace to those on whom his favour rests. The first announcement of Jesus' birth goes to shepherds — low-status, ritually unclean workers who couldn't keep Sabbath observances. God reveals the Messiah to outsiders first.

When the angels leave, the shepherds say to each other: let's go to Bethlehem and see this thing that has happened. They hurry and find Mary, Joseph, and the baby lying in the manger. After seeing him, they spread the word about what the angel told them. Everyone who hears it is amazed. Mary treasures all these things, pondering them in her heart. The shepherds return, glorifying and praising God for everything they've heard and seen. The shepherds become the first evangelists — they can't keep the news to themselves. Mary's response is different: quiet, internal, reflective. Luke likely got this detail from Mary herself.

On the eighth day, the child is circumcised and named Jesus — the name the angel gave before he was conceived. When the time of purification according to the Law of Moses is complete, Joseph and Mary take him to Jerusalem to present him to the Lord, as the law requires for every firstborn male. They offer a sacrifice: a pair of doves or two young pigeons — the offering prescribed for families who can't afford a lamb. Jesus' family follows the law meticulously. The dove offering tells us they're poor.

In Jerusalem there's a man named Simeon — righteous, devout, waiting for the consolation of Israel, and the Holy Spirit is on him. The Spirit has revealed to him that he won't die before he's seen the Lord's Messiah. Moved by the Spirit, he comes to the temple courts. When the parents bring in the child Jesus, Simeon takes him in his arms and praises God: Sovereign Lord, you may now dismiss your servant in peace. My eyes have seen your salvation, which you've prepared in the sight of all nations — a light for revelation to the Gentiles, and the glory of your people Israel. Joseph and Mary marvel at what's said. Then Simeon blesses them and tells Mary: this child is destined to cause the falling and rising of many in Israel, and to be a sign that will be spoken against, so that the thoughts of many hearts will be revealed. And a sword will pierce your own soul too. Simeon's prophecy is joyful and devastating in the same breath — salvation for the world, but suffering for the mother.

There's also a prophet named Anna, the daughter of Phanuel, of the tribe of Asher. She's very old — she lived with her husband seven years after her marriage and then was a widow until she was eighty-four. She never leaves the temple, worshipping night and day with fasting and prayer. Coming up at that very moment, she gives thanks to God and speaks about the child to all who are looking forward to the redemption of Jerusalem. Joseph and Mary do everything required by the law and return to Galilee, to Nazareth. The child grows, becomes strong, and is filled with wisdom, and the grace of God is on him. Two witnesses confirm who Jesus is: an old man and an old woman, both led by the Spirit, both waiting their whole lives for this moment.

Every year Jesus' parents go to Jerusalem for the Passover. When he's twelve, they go as usual. After the festival, they start home, but Jesus stays behind in Jerusalem without his parents knowing. Thinking he's in their group, they travel for a day before looking for him. When they don't find him, they return to Jerusalem. After three days they find him in the temple courts, sitting among the teachers, listening and asking questions. Everyone who hears him is amazed at his understanding and answers. His parents are astonished. Mary says: son, why have you treated us like this? Your father and I have been anxiously searching for you. Jesus replies: why were you searching for me? Didn't you know I had to be in my Father's house? They don't understand what he's saying. This is the only glimpse of Jesus between infancy and his public ministry. His first recorded words redefine his primary allegiance — from Joseph's house to his Father's house.

Jesus goes back to Nazareth with his parents and is obedient to them. Mary treasures all these things in her heart. Jesus grows in wisdom and stature, and in favour with God and man. Luke compresses roughly eighteen years into two sentences. Jesus develops normally — intellectually, physically, spiritually, socially. The Son of God goes through adolescence, learns a trade, and waits.
